coredata.dc:title: Additive manufacturing of highly fluorescent organic 3D-metastructures at sub-wavelength resolution
coredata.dc:creator: Jaiswal, A.
Rani, S.
Singh, G.P.
Hassan, M.
Nasrin, A.
Gomes, V.G.
Saxena, S.
Shukla, S.
coredata.dc:description: Functional metastructures exhibiting unique optical properties are desirable for advancements in microfluidics, optoelectronics, nonlinear photonics, biochemical sensing, and metamaterials. Additive manufacturing techniques are expected to enable rapid prototyping of complex 3D architectonics required for such applications. We report the fabrication of highly fluorescent all-organic metastructures via carbon quantum dot assisted in-situ polymerization of acrylate and carbon dot composite resin. The polymerized structures fabricated from acrylate and carbon dot composite resin exhibit excellent linear and nonlinear optical properties due to the presence of two-photon active carbon quantum dots incorporated in the resin. By activating the two-photon process, we eliminated the need for adding two-photon absorbing toxic chemicals for initiating the polymerization process. The fabrication of functional nanostructures using acrylate and carbon dot composite resin opens the door for rapid prototyping of metastructures for various applications such as developing functional metamaterials, solid-state lighting, live monitoring of cells in cultures, fluorescent tagging and detection of counterfeiting among others.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Additive manufacturing
Carbon quantum dot
Polymer composite
Photopolymerization
Metastructure

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of SR and ACCR

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Solar Energy Research Initiative (SERI), Government of India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the support from the joint collaboration agreements between IIT Bombay and The University of Sydney . The authors extend their gratitude to Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF) and Central Facility, Industrial Research and Consultancy Centre (IRCC) of Indian Institute of Technology, Bombay (IITB) for carrying out all the research activities. This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology , Solar Energy Research Initiative (SERI), Government of India grant via sanction order no. DST/TM/SERI/2k10/12/(G) .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing step by step processes involved in two-photon polymerization of ACCR. CDs upon two-photon excitation form active sites for polymerization. The monomer units react with the active sites and are attached to the CDs to form monomer radicals, which interact with other monomers, resulting in chain growth. The chain growth is terminated when two active sites interact with each other and bind, leading to the formation of dead polymer chains. The unit highlighted in yellow in the dead chain corresponds to the repeating unit, which repeats n times in the chain. The CDs remain attached to the polymer and serve as fluorescent probes. The array of fabricated pyramids (SEM scale: 10 μm) are shown with their 3D images reconstructed using stacked fluorescent images.

(a-b)Thin film of ACCR as seen under the microscope in brightfield transmission mode and fluorescent mode at an excitation of 405 nm, respectively; (c–d) Images of microgratings with a periodicity of 2.5 μm fabricated in ACCR as seen under brightfield transmission mode and fluorescent mode at an excitation of 405 nm, respectively; (e) Diffraction pattern obtained from microgratings using 532 nm diode laser; (f) Normalized emission intensity obtained from the fluorescent images of the microgratings, demonstrate uniform emissions from the fabricated lines and high signal to noise ratio; (g) 3D model of a micropyramid with large aspect ratio having base dimension a = b = 28 μm with layers represented at z = 0 μm, 20 μm and 40 μm, obtained using fluorescent imaging of the fabricated structure; (h–i) SEM images of the fabricated pyramid showing the top view and angular view (at 45°); (j) 3D reconstructed image of the fabricated micropyramid by stacking fluorescent images obtained at different heights.

head.ce:title: Additive manufacturing of highly fluorescent organic 3D-metastructures at sub-wavelength resolution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Functional metastructures exhibiting unique optical properties are desirable for advancements in microfluidics, optoelectronics, nonlinear photonics, biochemical sensing, and metamaterials. Additive manufacturing techniques are expected to enable rapid prototyping of complex 3D architectonics required for such applications. We report the fabrication of highly fluorescent all-organic metastructures via carbon quantum dot assisted in-situ polymerization of acrylate and carbon dot composite resin. The polymerized structures fabricated from acrylate and carbon dot composite resin exhibit excellent linear and nonlinear optical properties due to the presence of two-photon active carbon quantum dots incorporated in the resin. By activating the two-photon process, we eliminated the need for adding two-photon absorbing toxic chemicals for initiating the polymerization process. The fabrication of functional nanostructures using acrylate and carbon dot composite resin opens the door for rapid prototyping of metastructures for various applications such as developing functional metamaterials, solid-state lighting, live monitoring of cells in cultures, fluorescent tagging and detection of counterfeiting among others.

ce:section.ce:para: Functional metastructures at nano-micro scales are envisioned to comprise complex spatially ordered features in their structural embodiments. Spatial ordering of such metastructures by themselves can add unique functionalities to the materials, a concept, which has been widely exploited in the development of metamaterials. Incorporating additional layers of functionality is expected to add new dimensions for specialized applications of such metastructures. For example, optically-active, light-emitting metastructures at nano-micro scales are expected to enable the fabrication of smart devices for optical, micro-electromechanical, and biological applications such as solid-state lighting [1], miniature lasers [2, 3], optical waveguides [4,5], photonic bandgap materials [6,7], real-time monitoring of two-photon processes [8], probing of chemical reactions [9], bioimaging [10, 11], fluorescent stamping [12], and bionanoelectronics [13] to name a few. Luminescent materials such as metallic nanoparticles/nanoclusters [12,14], semiconductors [15] and carbon quantum dots [16], dyes [11], luminescent polymers [4] and semiconductor crystals doped with rare earth elements [17] have been developed over the past few decades in attempts to achieve these outcomes. However, the inability to achieve the desired morphology and long-range order has been the primary bottlenecks in this pursuit.

Additive manufacturing of complex fluorescent 3D-metastructures extends the possibilities of fabricating and integrating these nano-micro structures to produce optically active devices with desired functionalities. Attempts at fabrication of such devices have been made via self-assembly [6] and stamped assembly of polymer layers [18], capillary molding [2], direct laser writing [7,19], molecular imprinting [20,21], nanoimprinting [22], [23], [23], inkjet printing [24,25, 26], holographic projection [27] and optical lithography [28] among others. Optical lithography is of particular interest in this area, as this provides a choice of a wide spectrum of commercially available materials that can be doped with a variety of materials, such as organosilicone particles [29], graphene oxide modified TiO2 composite nanoparticles [30], polypyrroles [31], etc., to obtain tailored responses. However, the additional additives included in the UV curable resins increase the complexity of the photopolymerizable resins and may induce undesirable effects, hence, there are ongoing quests to develop minimal component systems. Several research groups have utilized nanoparticles based on zinc oxide [32,33, 34], titanium oxide [35], cadmium sulphide/selenide [36–39], and carbon [40–43] to induce photopolymerization, while exploiting the added benefits of tailored responses provided by these nanomaterials. However, the reported methods indicate the use of additional co-initiator molecules such as amines [38], alkyl halides [36], water [35,42] and usually require prolonged exposure times, making these formulations complex and unsuitable for rapid prototyping. Additionally, the fabrication process requires stringent control over the manufacturing parameters to achieve submicron resolution, while step-by-step, serial, time-consuming processing are undesired in additive manufacturing.

In-situ two-photon polymerization (TPP) is a promising candidate for 3D additive manufacturing of such nano-micro metastructures, due to the nonlinear character of two-photon processes, providing precise control over structural attributes to realize two and three-dimensional microstructures at subwavelength resolutions with complex geometries in a single exposure step [44] [–] [46]. Fabrication of fluorescent microstructures via two-photon lithography (TPL) hasten attempted primarily by using resins doped with dyes [47], luminescent polymers [4], metallic nanoparticles/salts [48], and semiconductor quantum dots [49], in addition to two-photon initiators. This not only increases the complexity of the fabrication process but also compromises the precision of the fabricated structures. The fluorescent chromophores on irradiation with femtosecond laser produces broadband emissions equivalent to single-photon excitation [50]; these emissions trigger unwanted polymerization, thus compromising the spatial resolution of the fabricated structures. Further, the moderate solubility of the additives in resins results in non-uniform staining of the microstructure, thereby, limiting the extent of doping with consequent aggregation of these molecules and leading to reduced emission intensities [9, 4]. With metal/semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) as dopants, difficulties with uniformly dispersing the QDs in the polymer resin are key bottlenecks. To mitigate these challenges, in-situ synthesis of quantum dots was explored to fabricate fluorescent microstructures. To achieve this objective, fabrication of CdS loaded fluorescent microstructures was attempted by in-situ reduction of the precursor. The fluorescence of these fabricated structures was dependent on the laser power used for fabrication, as the size of nanoparticles formed were dependent on the degree of cross-linking of the polymers [15]. In gold-loaded structures, the gold precursor was loaded into the resin, and subsequently post-fabrication heat-assisted reduction was performed, resulting in the formation of Au nanoparticles. The method unfortunately, lacked size control for the synthesized nanoparticles and resulted in uncontrolled emission from the fabricated structures [51]. The doped resins used in these studies required the incorporation of dedicated molecules in the resin to initiate photopolymerization and obtain desired emission characteristics in the structures. The incorporation of multiple molecules induces complexity in the fabrication process, thereby affecting the stability of the two-photon patternable material system (TPPMS). Furthermore, multiple materials in the TPPMS are not favorable for the monolithic integration of these functional structures. Recently, semiconductor and metal-semiconductor hybrid (CdS/CdS–Au) nanoparticles were used as photoinitiators for single/two-photon polymerization in aqueous solution [52] and additive-free fluorescent structures were obtained using CdSe/CdS seeded nanorods as initiators [53]. Although the formulations were devoid of any additives, the use of CdSe/CdS nanomaterials severely restricts the applicability of this technology, especially in bio-application due to their cytotoxicity. Further, the synthesis of CdSe/CdS nanoparticles involve complex and time-consuming processes [54]. More importantly, the highest resolutions demonstrated in their work were of the order of micrometres. These drawbacks indicate an urgent need to develop an additive-free, solvent-free resin with sufficient TPP sensitivity, to enable a simple one-step method to additively fabricate functional/functionalized all-organic metastructures having sub-wavelength resolution.

Here, we report a novel method to fabricate all organic metastructures by incorporating carbon dots (CDs) in acrylate resins. The CDs in the TPPMS are able to trigger photopolymerization and simultaneously serve as fluorescent markers in the polymerized structure. Optically-active two and three-dimensional microstructures with submicron resolution were fabricated by femtosecond laser radiation in our CD-based TPPMS. A bottom-up approach was taken to synthesize two-photon active CDs by using hydrothermal technique. The powdered CDs so obtained were mixed with the acrylate resin to obtain the TPPMS. The optical activity of the CDs was used to induce two-photon processes and to stain the microstructures for fluorescence measurements. The fluorescence characteristics and stability of TPPMS were studied in detail using laser scanning and optical microscopes. The emission intensities were found to be dependent on the CD loading and were found to increase with increasing CD concentration in the acrylate and carbon dot composite resin (ACCR). The fabricated structures on characterization confirmed the high quality of the structures obtained in terms of their dimensional stability and replication fidelity. The optical activity of the CDs remained preserved in the fabricated microstructures. Functional gratings with subwavelength resolution were fabricated in ACCR. The method developed provides a promising solution for additively manufacturing functional, biocompatible, fluorescent, all-organic metastructures in a single lithography step and is expected to open new avenues in optical technologies along with non-toxic attributes for biomedical applications.

ACCR was formulated by adding hydrothermally synthesized, oxygen-rich two-photon active CDs to Sartomer ® Resin (SR), which comprised a mixture of two triacrylate monomers in 1:1 (w/w) proportion: tris-(2-hydroxyethyl) isocyanurate triacrylate (SR386) and ethoxylated trimethylolpropane triacrylate (SR499). Further, ACCR was spin casted on a glass coverslip and irradiated with femtosecond pulsed laser. The operating wavelength for fabrication was set to 800 nm, owing to the best non-linear optical response (two-photon emission) of CDs at 800 nm, when examined in the NIR range 720 nm–880 nm. A strong fluorescence band centered around 520 nm was observed for CD under the excitation of 800 nm laser, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S1. Upon two-photon excitation, radicals were generated on the surface of CD, which serve as the active sites for initiating cross-linking of the photopolymer. Following the interaction of acrylate moieties with the active sites, monomer radicals were generated. These radicals in turn interact with other acrylate moieties present in the vicinity to maintain chain propagation. The termination of chain growth was facilitated by the interaction of other active sites or monomer radicals conjugated throughout the process, resulting instabilized polymer voxels. We note that the oxy-rich surface states of the CDs are consumed during polymerization, and is evident from the high-resolution XPS spectra (C1s and O1s) of SR, CD and ACCR, shown in Supplementary Fig. S2 (a-f). The reduction in free surface states of CDs reduces the probability of relaxation via non-radiative routes. Hence, the CDs embedded in the nanostructures essentially act as arrays of fluorophores and on excitation relax via radiative decay.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of SR and ACCR
ce:section.ce:para: Variable concentrations of CDs ranging from 0.1 to 20 wt% were loaded in SR and their fluorescence characteristics were evaluated. SR is transparent, colourless resin but addition of CDs imparted brownish tint to the resin as shown in Supplementary Fig. S3 (a) and (b) respectively. The microdroplets of ACCR with different wt% of CD loading were examined under white light and ultraviolet (UV) lamp (360–380 nm, 3 mW). The optical transparency of the resin was found to reduce with increasing CD concentration. The lower threshold level for CD loading of ACCR to obtain fluorescent structures was found to be ∼2.5 wt% as shown in Supplementary Figs. S3 (a) and (b) for white and UV light, respectively. Clear, bright green emission was observed from the microdroplet containing 2.5 wt% CD while the other microdroplets appeared bluish due to UV illumination, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S3 (d). Under two-photon excitation (800 nm), ACCR2.5 exhibited bright green emission, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S3 (e). The absorbance and fluorescence behaviour of SR andACCR were evaluated. The SR resin has no absorption in the visible region and ACCR resin exhibits absorption over a wide range from 275 nm to 500 nm, due to the addition of CDs. Two major peaks centered around 277 nm and 340 nm were observed in the UV–vis absorption spectra of ACCR, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S4 (a), these can be attributed to the π–π∗ transition of sp2 carbons mainly in the core of CDs and the n–π∗ transition of oxy-rich groups on the surface states of CDs, respectively. SR is a non-emissive resin, but ACCR has a strong emission under single-photon excitation at 400 nm, shows a peak centered around 510 nm, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S4 (b). The study of the impact of CD loading on the emission intensity revealed the enhancement of emission intensity with increasing CD loading, as shown in Supplementary Fig. S4(c).

Prior to fabrication, the spin-cast film of ACCR20 (ACCR with 20 wt% CD) was examined under an optical microscope to check the quality of the film. Optical transmission image of the film showed a uniform film structure of good quality, as shown in Fig. 2 (a) and under 405 nm laser irradiation (3 mW power) the ACCR20 film exhibited bright green emission. False color image of the film under UV illumination is shown in Fig. 2(b) which reveals uniform emission over the illuminated region and hence homogenous distribution of CDs within the film.

Microscopic investigation of the fabricated two-dimensional micrograting was conducted for morphological characterization. The optical transmission images show a section of the micrograting with a periodicity of 2.5 μm and lines with a lateral resolution of ∼300 nm, revealing the high fidelity and reproducibility of the features in the structure as seen in Fig. 2(c). The micrograting was further examined using UV laser illumination at 405 nm and 3 mW power. The structures exhibited properties similar to those of the ACCR20 resin in the form of bright green and uniform emissions. Fig. 2(d) shows a false-color fluorescent image of the micrograting, illustrating the precise control and reproducibility of the TPL fabrication method. The average emission intensity obtained from the fluorescent lines shows uniform emission throughout the structure and a high signal to background noise ratio. The fluorescent intensity can be clearly seen in the plot of intensities extracted from the fluorescent region and the background, represented by the peaks and flat regions, respectively (Fig. 2(f)). This confirms the uniform staining of the structures. The distinct spots in the diffraction pattern, obtained from the gratings, shown in Fig. 2(e), represent the uniformity of the structure over the entire construct, and zoomed-in view of a single line of the grating shown in SEM micrograph, Supplementary Fig. S5 (b) reveals the subwavelength resolution (∼300 nm) for the fabricated structure.

A 3 × 3 array of pyramid structures with varying aspect ratios of base edge and height were fabricated. The 3D model of a single element of the array, with a height of 40 μm and base edge of 28 μm is shown in Fig. 2(g). The three layers of the pyramid at z = 0, 20 and 40 μm, obtained by fluorescent imaging at different depths have been overlapped with the 3D model. Fig. 2(h and i) shows scanning electron micrographs of the top view and the angular view of the fabricated structure. The micrographs suggest precise control of dimension and architecture. The 3D reconstructed fluorescent image of the pyramid (Fig. 2(j)), shows the exact reproduction of the features of the fabricated structure, providing the added benefit of non-invasive, in-depth imaging of the fabricated structures. The top profile and angular views were acquired using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and reconstructed using fluorescent image stacking for the entire pyramid array with their detailed dimensions as shown in Supplementary Fig. S6& S7, respectively. The top views and the three-dimensional views at different heights were reconstructed using false color fluorescent images as shown in Supplementary Fig. S8. The images reveal excellent quality and reproducibility of features in their structures. Since ACCR is two-photon active, two-photon fluorescent imaging is also viable for revealing finer features fabricated in ACCR.

We have developed a novel method for additive manufacturing of highly fluorescent all-organic 3D-metastructures with subwavelength resolution as a potential process for rapid prototyping of functional metastructures. Both 2D- and 3D-metastructures with high aspect ratios were fabricated using the novel formulation of CDs incorporated in ACCR. The two-photon active CDs not only act as chromophores but also serve as a photoinitiator for the two-photon polymerization process, resulting in one-step lithography of fluorescent metastructures. CD incorporated TPPMS exhibited excellent TPP sensitivity, and fabricated metastructures fabricated using this method exhibited subwavelength features of ∼300 nm. Results based on scanning as well as fluorescence micrographs display high degree of precision and dimensional stability in fabricated structures. Further optimization of process parameters is expected to result in further improvement in resolution. Our developed method of additive manufacturing of 2D- and 3D-metastructures is expected to create a paradigm shift in rapid prototyping of much-needed functional metastructures for the development of miniaturized functional devices.

The CDs were synthesized [55] using a bottom-up hydrothermal method. 0.5 g of polyphenol-based organic precursor, which was dispersed in 50 mL water by sonicating for about 5 min. The mixture was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon vessel which was sealed within a stainless-steel autoclave chamber. The autoclave was loaded into an oven and heated at 240 °C for 4 h. On completion of the hydrothermal reaction, the assembly was allowed to cool down to room temperature. The suspension from the reaction was collected and subjected to centrifugation at 14,000 RCF for 30 min. The supernatant was further processed by ultrafiltration via dialysis (MWCO of 30 kDa) for 30 min at 5000 RPM. The stock solution having a quantitative yield of 72 wt% was lyophilized and stored in the refrigerator at 4 °C for characterization and subsequent application.

The SR solution was prepared by mixing two triacrylate monomers:tris-(2-hydroxyethyl) isocyanurate (SR368) and the ethoxylated (6) trimethylolpropane triacrylate (SR499) in 1:1 mass ratio. SR368 and SR499 were used as received from Sartomer. Powdered CDs were added to the SR resin and the mixture was sonicated for 2 h to ensure homogenous mixing of CDs. ACCR containing 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, 2.5 wt%, 5 wt%, 10 wt% and 20 wt% CDs were prepared and designated as ACCR0.5, ACCR1, ACCR2.5, ACCR5, ACCR10 and ACCR20, respectively. The formulated resins were stored in a vacuum desiccator and placed in the dark for further use.

A uniform thin film of ACCR20 was spin-coated on clean glass coverslips. A femtosecond pulsed beam, operating at a repetition rate of 80 MHz with a duration of 140 fs from a laser gun (Coherent Chameleon, Ultra I) was tightly focused into the ACCR using a 100X, 0.9 NA objective lens to induce pin-point polymerization in the irradiated focal volume. Subsequently, the sample was traversed with reference to the fixed focus, using a precision xyz translation stage assembly of a piezo-controlled stage coupled to stepper motor controlled stages (Physik Instrumente GmbH &Co. KG) in order to fabricate the structures. The traversal stages were controlled using an integrated LabVIEW code. The resin in the unexposed region was washed away by rinsing with DMF (dimethyl formamide) for further characterization. The fabricated structures were characterized using optical (Olympus-IX81) and fluorescence (Carl Zeiss LSM 780) microscopy.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Co-authors MH and VG are inventors on an Australian Patent Application No. 2020901760 titled “Nanoparticle conjugates and their use in the therapeutic applications” filed on May 29, 2020.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support from the joint collaboration agreements between IIT Bombay and The University of Sydney. The authors extend their gratitude to Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility (SAIF) and Central Facility, Industrial Research and Consultancy Centre (IRCC) of Indian Institute of Technology, Bombay (IITB) for carrying out all the research activities. This work was supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Solar Energy Research Initiative (SERI), Government of India grant via sanction order no. DST/TM/SERI/2k10/12/(G).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mtphys.2021.100434.
The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time as the data also forms part of an ongoing study.

Arun Jaiswal:Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing review and edit, Sweta Rani: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing review and edit, Gaurav Pratap Singh:Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing review and edit, Mahbub Hassan:Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Validation, Visualization, Writing review and edit, Aklima Nasrin; Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Vincent G.Gomes: Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing review and edit, Sumit Saxena: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing review and edit, Shobha Shukla: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing review and edit.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescence properties of Na3SrB5O10:Dy3+ plate-like microstructures for solid state lighting applications

Duan, Inhibited/enhanced fluorescence of embedded fluorescent defects by manipulation of spontaneous emission based on photonic stopband, RSC Adv. 7 (2017) 19737-19741. https://doi.org/10.1039/C7RA02269G.

Gadonas, Organic dye doped microstructures for optically active functional devices fabricated via two-photon polymerization technique, Lith. J. Phys. 50 (2010) 55-61. https://doi.org/10.3952/lithjphys.50112.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Three-dimensional fabrication of optically active microstructures containing an electroluminescent polymer

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoparticle-mediated fluorescence enhancement by two-photon polymerized 3D microstructures

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembled conjugated polymer spheres as fluorescent microresonators

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct laser writing of photostable fluorescent silver nanoclusters in polymer films

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emission features of microstructures fabricated by two-photon polymerization containing three organic dyes
